12 Step Christianity.html:
The table of contents for 12-Step Christianity: The Gospel and the Twelve Steps by Saul Selby is structured to bridge the gap between traditional Christian theology (the Gospel) and the recovery principles of the Twelve Steps. Table of Contents

  • Acknowledgments
  • Introduction: The Gospel and the Twelve Steps
  • Part One: The Basics
    • The Twelve Steps: A Bridge to the Gospel
    • The Language of Recovery
  • Part Two: The Twelve Steps
    • Step 1: Powerlessness and Sin
    • ▪ Coming to terms with our inability to save ourselves.
    • Step 2: Hope and the Power of God
    • ▪ Believing that a Power greater than ourselves can restore us.
    • Step 3: Decision and Surrender
    • ▪ Turning our will and our lives over to the care of God.
    • Step 4: Self-Examination and Truth
    • ▪ Making a searching and fearless moral inventory.
    • Step 5: Confession and Forgiveness
    • ▪ Admitting to God, ourselves, and another human being the exact nature of our wrongs.
    • Step 6: Willingness and Change
    • ▪ Being entirely ready for God to remove all these defects of character.
    • Step 7: Humility and Transformation
    • ▪ Humbly asking Him to remove our shortcomings.
    • Step 8: Responsibility and Relationships
    • ▪ Making a list of all persons we have harmed and becoming willing to make amends.
    • Step 9: Amends and Reconciliation
    • ▪ Making direct amends wherever possible.
    • Step 10: Vigilance and Growth
    • ▪ Continuing to take personal inventory and admitting when we are wrong.
    • Step 11: Prayer and Meditation
    • ▪ Seeking through prayer and meditation to improve our conscious contact with God.
    • Step 12: Witness and Service
    • ▪ Carrying the message to others and practicing these principles in all our affairs.
  • Part Three: The Journey Continues
    • Living the Gospel and the Twelve Steps
    • The Church and the Twelve Steps
  • Notes
  • Bibliography
  • Core Approach
  • Selby's work is often used by individuals and church groups to show that the Twelve Steps are not just for clinical recovery, but are deeply rooted in biblical concepts like:
  • Repentance (Steps 1–3): Acknowledging the need for God.
  • Sanctification (Steps 4–10): The ongoing process of character transformation.
  • Service (Steps 11–12): Living out the faith in community.
  • This book provides a theological framework for those who find the language of "Higher Power" too vague and want to specifically integrate their faith in Jesus Christ with their recovery journey.

2 Timothy.author: NavPress
2 Timothy.html:
The "LifeChange" series by The Navigators (published by NavPress) is designed for deep, inductive Bible study. Their study on 2 Timothy is structured into 8 lessons that cover the entire book, along with introductory historical material. Table of Contents: 2 Timothy (LifeChange Series)

  • How to Use This Study
  • Introduction: Historical Background (Paul and Timothy)
  • Map of the Roman Empire
  • Timeline of Paul’s Ministry
  • Lesson 1: Overview
    • Getting the "big picture" of the letter.
  • Lesson 2: Unashamed (1:1–14)
    • Focus: Fanning the gift into flame and the spirit of power.
  • Lesson 3: Endurance (1:15–2:13)
    • Focus: Being strengthened by grace and enduring hardship like a good soldier.
  • Lesson 4: A Workman Approved (2:14–26)
    • Focus: Correctly handling the word of truth and avoiding pointless chatter.
  • Lesson 5: Rejectors of Truth (3:1–9)
    • Focus: Navigating "terrible times" and recognizing those with a form of godliness.
  • Lesson 6: Timothy’s Guides (3:10–17)
    • Focus: The importance of mentors and the God-breathed nature of Scripture.
  • Lesson 7: Passing the Mantle (4:1–8)
    • Focus: Paul’s final charge to preach the word and his reflection on finishing the race.
  • Lesson 8: Personal Needs and Review (4:9–22)
    • Focus: Paul’s final requests, greetings, and a summary review of the book's themes.
  • Study Features
  • Each lesson typically includes:
  • Word Origins: Exploring the original Koine Greek definitions for key terms (which aligns well with your interest in learning the language).
  • Historical Insights: Context about the Roman prison system and the church at Ephesus.
  • Application: "Your Response" sections for personal reflection.
  • Going Deeper: Optional projects for further study.

A Cry for Mercy.author: Nouwen
A Cry for Mercy.html:
The table of contents for Henri J.M. Nouwen’s "A Cry for Mercy: Prayers from the Genesee" reflects his seven-month stay at the Abbey of the Genesee. The book is structured as a six-week spiritual retreat, with seven prayers for each week. Table of Contents

  • Preface
  • Introduction: The Journey to the Genesee
  • The First Week: A Fearful Heart
    • Focus: Facing internal anxieties and the struggle to pray.
  • The Second Week: A Cry for Mercy
    • Focus: Recognizing the need for God's grace and forgiveness.
  • The Third Week: Rays of Hope
    • Focus: Finding light amidst spiritual darkness.
  • The Fourth Week: The Power of the Spirit
    • Focus: Relying on the Holy Spirit for strength and guidance.
  • The Fifth Week: The Needs of the World
    • Focus: Intercessory prayer and looking outward beyond the self.
  • The Sixth Week: Gratitude
    • Focus: Offering thanks for the gifts received during the retreat.
  • Conclusion: The Return from the Genesee

A Family Album.author: Swindoll
A Family Album.html:
Yes, there is a work by Charles R. Swindoll (often referred to as Chuck Swindoll) titled "A Family Album: Biblical Snapshots for Unique Situations." It is primarily structured as a series of character studies—or "snapshots"—from the Bible, often sold as a book or a study guide accompanied by audio messages. Table of Contents (Message Outline) The book is organized into eight chapters, each focusing on a specific biblical figure or family dynamic:

  • Meet the Mother of Moses
  • A look at Jochebed and the courage of motherhood.
  • A Couple Mightily Used of God
  • An exploration of Aquila and Priscilla’s partnership.
  • “Grandpa Uzziah”: A Study in Contrasts
  • Analyzing the rise and fall of King Uzziah and his legacy.
  • The Parents of a Son Born Blind
  • Addressing the challenges and faith of parents in difficult circumstances.
  • A Double Blessing for a Single Parent
  • Encouragement and biblical perspective for single-parent households.
  • Earning the Right to Be Called “Dad”
  • Focusing on the responsibilities and heart of fatherhood.
  • From Captive to Queen: An Adoption Story
  • The story of Esther and Mordecai through the lens of adoption.
  • If You Have the Solo . . . Sing Out!
  • A concluding message on individual purpose and faithful living.
  • Like many of Swindoll’s works, this "album" uses the metaphor of a photo collection to examine the diverse and often messy reality of families throughout history.

A Life God Rewards.html:
The table of contents for "A Life God Rewards: Why Everything You Do Today Matters Forever" by Bruce Wilkinson (the author of The Prayer of Jabez) is organized into eight concise chapters. The book is part of his "Breakthrough Series" and focuses on the biblical concept of eternal rewards based on present-day actions. Table of Contents

  • Chapter 1: Keyhole to the Stars
    • Introduction to the idea that our "blink in the sun" (life on earth) determines our "endless day" (eternity).
  • Chapter 2: The Unbreakable Link
    • Explaining the direct connection between your behavior today and your experience in heaven.
  • Chapter 3: What the Bible Says about Rewards
    • A foundational look at scriptural evidence for degrees of reward.
  • Chapter 4: That Day
    • Focusing on the "Bema Seat" or the judgment seat of Christ.
  • Chapter 5: The Question of Your Life
    • Identifying the central motivation behind your daily decisions.
  • Chapter 6: The God Who Gives Back
    • A study on the character of God as a generous rewarder.
  • Chapter 7: The First Key
    • Practical steps to begin living with an eternal perspective.
  • Chapter 8: Seeing through to Forever
    • A concluding reflection on how this shift in mindset changes your daily life.
  • Note on Other Editions
  • Because this book is often used for group study, you may encounter different structures for its companion materials:
  • The Bible Study: Usually structured as a four-week or six-week curriculum.
  • The Teen Edition: Contains similar themes but often includes a specific section titled "A Billion Years from Now."

A Life Well Lived.author: Swindoll
A Life Well Lived.html:
Given your interest in Charles Swindoll, you are likely looking for his 2007 book (part of the "Message of Hope" series), which focuses on the book of Micah. However, there are a few other notable spiritual and biographical works with this exact title.

  • A Life Well Lived
  • By Charles R. Swindoll (2007)
  • This book is a focused study on the Old Testament prophet Micah and his famous call to "do justice, love mercy, and walk humbly."
  • Chapter 1: A Minor Prophet with a Major Message
  • Chapter 2: God Expects Us to Love Kindness
  • Chapter 3: God Expects Us to Walk Humbly with Him
  • Chapter 4: The Reward of a Life Well Lived
  • A Life Well Lived: The Best of Jim Elliot
  • Compiled by Elisabeth Elliot
  • Jim Elliot was a famous missionary whose journals and letters were compiled by his wife, Elisabeth. While the journals are chronological, the "Life Well Lived" compilations usually follow this thematic structure:
  • Introduction: The Young Man and His Mission
  • Part I: The Call of the Wild (Early years and training)
  • Part II: To the Edge of the World (Journey to Ecuador)
  • Part III: The Aucas (Preparation for the mission to the Huaorani people)
  • Part IV: The Ultimate Sacrifice (The events of Palm Beach)
  • Conclusion: He Is No Fool (Based on his most famous quote)
  • The 12 Essentials of Godly Success: Biblical Steps to a Life Well Lived
  • By Tommy Nelson
  • If you are looking for a more comprehensive "success-oriented" Christian book, this one is often referred to by its subtitle:
  • 1. The Ability to Work and Play Well with Others
  • 2. What Do You Treasure?
  • 3. Matching Up Your Faith and Life
  • 4. Subduing Your Flesh through the Spirit
  • 5. Success in the Place God Plants You
  • 6. Rolling with the Punches of Life
  • 7. Who Will You Allow to Influence Your Heart?
  • 8. The Sacredness of the Secular
  • 9. Foundation for Joy or Rock in Your Shoe?
  • 10. Eight Essential Vitamins for Your Kids
  • 11. Handle or Be Handled by Wealth
  • Conclusion: Mastering Life

A Shepherd Looks at Psalm 23.author: Keller
A Shepherd Looks at Psalm 23.html:
"A Shepherd Looks at Psalm 23" by W. Phillip Keller (first published in 1970) is a classic devotional that interprets each phrase of the Psalm through the author's personal experience as a literal shepherd in East Africa. The book is structured into 12 chapters, each corresponding to a specific portion of the Psalm: Table of Contents

  • Chapter 1: “The Lord Is My Shepherd”
  • Chapter 2: “I Shall Not Want”
  • Chapter 3: “He Maketh Me to Lie Down in Green Pastures”
  • Chapter 4: “He Leadeth Me Beside the Still Waters”
  • Chapter 5: “He Restoreth My Soul”
  • Chapter 6: “He Leadeth Me in the Paths of Righteousness for His Name’s Sake”
  • Chapter 7: “Yea, Though I Walk through the Valley of the Shadow of Death, I Will Fear No Evil: For Thou Art with Me”
  • Chapter 8: “Thy Rod and Thy Staff They Comfort Me”
  • Chapter 9: “Thou Preparest a Table Before Me in the Presence of Mine Enemies”
  • Chapter 10: “Thou Anointest My Head with Oil”
  • Chapter 11: “My Cup Runneth Over”
  • Chapter 12: “Surely Goodness and Mercy Shall Follow Me All the Days of My Life and I Will Dwell in the House of the Lord Forever”
  • Why the Structure Matters
  • Keller’s unique contribution is explaining the agricultural reality behind the metaphors. For example:
  • "Green Pastures": He explains that in arid climates, green pastures don't happen by chance; they are the result of the shepherd's intensive labor in clearing rocks and irrigating land.
  • "The Rod and Staff": He details the specific functional differences between the rod (a weapon for protection) and the staff (a tool for management and gentle guidance).
  • "Anointest My Head with Oil": He describes how shepherds use oil to protect sheep from the "nose fly" and other parasites that can drive a flock to distraction.
  • This focus on the physical environment of the shepherd often provides a vivid backdrop for those interested in the historical and practical context of biblical imagery.

Adventuring with God.author: Swindoll
Adventuring with God.html:
The table of contents for Adventuring with God: Following in the Apostles' Footsteps by Charles R. Swindoll corresponds to his teaching series and study guide focused on the book of Acts. This series explores the early church's growth and the "adventure" of living under the guidance of the Holy Spirit. Table of Contents

  • 1. Reaching Our World
    • Focusing on the transition from Jesus' ascension to the mission of the apostles (Acts 1:1–11).
  • 2. Birthing the Church
    • The arrival of the Holy Spirit at Pentecost and the beginning of the global movement (Acts 2:1–13).
  • 3. Touching Others’ Lives
    • The first miracle after Pentecost—the healing of the lame man—and the power of spiritual authority (Acts 3:1–11).
  • 4. Being Transformed
    • The radical conversion of Saul on the road to Damascus (Acts 9:1–19).
  • 5. Prevailing through Prayer
    • A look at Peter’s miraculous release from prison through the persistent prayer of the church (Acts 12:1–17).
  • 6. Adjusting to Change
    • How the early church navigated transitions and the Holy Spirit’s leading into new missions (Acts 13:1–4).
  • 7. The Spirit Who Is Not a Ghost
    • Understanding the personhood and presence of the Holy Spirit in the believer's life.
  • 8. Cultivating a Spirit-Controlled Life
    • Practical principles for yielding to the Spirit's influence daily.
  • 9. From Agony to Ecstasy
    • Finding God's purpose and joy even through trials and persecution.
  • 10. The Glory of God
    • The ultimate goal of the Christian adventure: reflecting God's character to the world.
  • Key Themes
  • Swindoll defines the Christian life not as a "safe routine" but as a "serendipitous adventure" directed by God. The book/study guide emphasizes:
  • Sensitivity: Learning to hear and obey God’s promptings.
  • Availability: Being ready to go where God leads, even when it’s inconvenient.
  • Power: Relying on the Holy Spirit rather than human effort to witness to others.
  • This resource is often used for small group studies or personal reflection to encourage believers to step out of their comfort zones and trust God's sovereign hand.

Aging.author: Nouwen
Aging.html:
The table of contents for Aging: The Fulfillment of Life by Henri J.M. Nouwen and Walter J. Gaffney is structured into two primary parts that explore both the internal experience of growing older and the communal responsibility of caring for the elderly. Table of Contents

  • Prologue
  • Introduction
  • Part I: Aging
    • 1. Aging as a Way to the Darkness
    • ▪ Segregation: The social rejection of the elderly.
    • ▪ Desolation: The emotional pain of being left behind.
    • ▪ Loss of Self: The struggle with a fading identity.
    • 2. Aging as a Way to the Light
    • ▪ From Wishes to Hope: Shifting focus from specific desires to a broader spiritual trust.
    • ▪ From Uselessness to Purity of Heart: Finding value in "being" rather than just "doing."
    • Conclusion to Part I
  • Part II: Caring
    • 3. Caring as a Way to Compassion
    • ▪ The Nature of Care: Understanding that caring for the aging is a way to confront our own mortality.
    • ▪ The Patient as Teacher: How the elderly offer wisdom to the community.
    • ▪ Intergenerational Community: Rebuilding the connections between the young and the old.
  • Conclusion
  • Core Philosophy
  • Nouwen and Gaffney present aging not as a "slow decaying" to be feared, but as a "gradual maturing" and a "chance to be embraced."
  • Vulnerability: The authors argue that by acknowledging our own aging and limitations, we become more compassionate and present to others.
  • Hope vs. Wishes: A key theme is the distinction between wishes (which are tied to specific outcomes) and hope (which is a state of the heart that trusts in God regardless of the outcome).
  • Visual Element: The book is famous for including 85 photographs that serve as a visual meditation alongside the text, emphasizing the beauty found in every stage of life.
  • This book is frequently cited in pastoral care and spirituality for its gentle, reflective approach to a topic often met with anxiety.

Between Heaven and Hell.html:
The table of contents for Between Heaven and Hell: A Dialog Somewhere Beyond Death by Peter Kreeft is unique because the book is written as a play-style Socratic dialogue rather than a collection of traditional chapters. The book imagines a conversation between C.S. Lewis (representing Christian Theism), John F. Kennedy (representing Modern Humanism), and Aldous Huxley (representing Eastern Pantheism)—all of whom died on the same day: November 22, 1963. Table of Contents

  • Prologue
    • Setting the scene and introducing the three men as they find themselves in a "limbo-like" state after death.
  • The Dialog
    • The core of the book. While not divided into formal chapters, the conversation moves through several major philosophical movements:
    • ▪ The Nature of Truth: Is truth relative or objective?
    • ▪ The Identity of Jesus: A debate on Lewis's "Liar, Lunatic, or Lord" trilemma.
    • ▪ Worldviews: Comparing Western Christianity, Secular Humanism, and Eastern Mysticism.
    • ▪ Life after Death: What it means to be "in-between."
  • Epilogue
    • A concluding reflection on the ultimate fate of the characters and the weight of their arguments.
  • Postscript (Included in the Anniversary/Signature Editions)
    • Kreeft shares his personal reasons for writing the book and his thoughts on the three men.
  • Appendix A: A World Without an Easter
    • An additional dialogue imagining a world where the Resurrection never happened.
  • Appendix B: Outline to Between Heaven and Hell
    • A logical breakdown of the arguments presented in the dialog.
  • Index
  • Why the Structure Matters

Beyond the Mirror.html:
The table of contents for Beyond the Mirror: Reflections on Death and Life by Henri J.M. Nouwen follows the narrative of his near-death experience in 1989. After being hit by a van while walking on an icy road, Nouwen used the recovery period to reflect on the "portal of death" and what it taught him about life and faith. Table of Contents

  • Foreword (by Robert Durback)
  • Prologue
    • The context of the accident and Nouwen's state of mind leading up to it.
  • The Accident
    • A detailed account of the event on the icy highway in Ontario.
  • The Hospital
    • The immediate aftermath and the realization of the severity of his injuries.
  • The Surgery
    • Reflections on the "portal of death" and the experience of peace and unconditional love he felt while close to death.
  • Recovery
    • The physical and spiritual journey back to health and the struggle with returning to "normal" life.
  • Epilogue
    • Concluding thoughts on the permanent shift in his perspective on ministry and identity.
  • Preparing for Death
    • A concluding reflection on how to live in a way that prepares one for a "finely finished" death.
  • Key Themes
  • The book is a short, intimate memoir (approximately 96 pages) that explores:
  • The Portal of Death: Nouwen describes death not as a dark hole but as a doorway to a light-filled presence where he felt completely accepted.
  • Unfinished Business: He honestly recounts how his initial peace was interrupted by the realization of people he had not yet fully forgiven, turning his recovery into a journey of reconciliation.
  • Vocation as Witness: He concludes that his primary calling is to be a "witness to the glimpses of God" he was allowed to catch during those critical moments.

Bible Books Summary.html:
Because "Bible Books Summary" is a common title for many different study guides and reference charts, most follow the canonical structure of the Bible itself. Whether you are looking at a handbook or a set of reference cards, the table of contents is typically organized into these major divisions: The Old Testament (39 Books) The Pentateuch (Books of the Law)

  • Genesis: Origins and the Covenant.
  • Exodus: Deliverance and the Law.
  • Leviticus: Holiness and Ritual.
  • Numbers: Wilderness Wanderings.
  • Deuteronomy: The Law Rehearsed.
  • Historical Books
  • Pre-Kingdom: Joshua, Judges, Ruth.
  • The Kingdom: 1 & 2 Samuel, 1 & 2 Kings, 1 & 2 Chronicles.
  • Post-Exile: Ezra, Nehemiah, Esther.
  • Poetic & Wisdom Literature
  • Job: Suffering and Sovereignty.
  • Psalms: Praise and Prayer.
  • Proverbs: Practical Wisdom.
  • Ecclesiastes: The Meaning of Life.
  • Song of Solomon: Celebration of Love.

  • Major Prophets: Isaiah, Jeremiah, Lamentations, Ezekiel, Daniel.
  • Minor Prophets: The Twelve (Hosea through Malachi).
  • The New Testament (27 Books)
  • The Gospels & Early Church History

  • The Fourth Gospel: John.
  • History: Acts (The Growth of the Church).
  • The Epistles (Letters)
  • Pauline Epistles: Romans, 1 & 2 Corinthians, Galatians, Ephesians, Philippians, Colossians, 1 & 2 Thessalonians.
  • Pastoral Epistles: 1 & 2 Timothy, Titus, Philemon.
  • General Epistles: Hebrews, James, 1 & 2 Peter, 1, 2, & 3 John, Jude.
  • Prophecy
  • Revelation: The Final Vision and the New Creation.
  • Common Summary Features
  • Most "Bible Book Summary" resources will include the following for each entry:
  • Author & Date: Who wrote it and when.
  • Key Themes: The primary theological focus.
  • Key Verses: The "memory verses" of that book.
  • Christ in the Book: How the book points toward the Messiah.
  • Outline: A brief 3–5 point breakdown of the book's narrative.

Life Connections.author: God
Life Connections.html:
"Life Connections" usually refers to the CSB Life Connections Study Bible (formerly the Serendipity Study Bible). Rather than a standard narrative table of contents, this resource is organized by the books of the Bible, featuring thousands of study notes and over 200 specialized study courses. If you are looking at the CSB Life Connections Study Bible, the "Table of Contents" is structured to help you navigate its unique small-group and personal study features:

  • Front Matter
  • Introduction to the CSB Life Connections Study Bible
  • The "Open-Consider-Apply" Method: An explanation of the three-step study process used throughout the margins.
  • How to Use This Bible for Small Groups: Guidance on facilitating 60-minute or 90-minute sessions.
  • Topical Study Courses (The Heart of the Bible)
  • The Bible includes hundreds of "Courses"—reading plans that jump through different passages to cover a specific theme. The Table of Contents for these courses is typically indexed by:
  • 7-Week Courses: For new believers or short-term groups (e.g., "The Life of Christ," "Basic Christian Beliefs").
  • 13-Week Courses: For deeper quarterly studies (e.g., "Marriage and Family," "Work and Career," "The Miracles of Jesus").
  • Course Index by Topic: Grouped by life stages (Men, Women, Students, Seekers, etc.).
  • The Books of the Bible (Canonical Order)
  • Each book (from Genesis to Revelation) acts as a chapter and includes:
  • Book Introduction: Background on the author, date, and historical context.
  • The Margin Study Questions:
    • Open: Icebreaker questions to start a conversation.
    • Consider: Observation questions focusing on what the text says.
    • Apply: Transformational questions about how to live the truth.
  • Dig Deeper Notes: More technical or historical information on specific verses.
  • Back Matter
  • Topical Index: A guide to finding specific subjects (e.g., Grief, Forgiveness, Purpose).
  • Concordance: A tool for finding verses based on keywords.
  • Maps & Charts: Visual aids for biblical geography and timelines.
  • The "Life Connections" Workbook Series
  • If you are referring to the individual Life Connections study guides (published by Lifeway/Serendipity), these are separate workbooks for specific books of the Bible (like Romans or 1 & 2 Samuel). Each workbook typically follows this 7-to-13 session structure:
  • Session 1: The Call/Introduction
  • Sessions 2–12: Passage-by-Passage Analysis (using the Open-Consider-Apply method)
  • Final Session: Summary and Commitment
  • Which version are you using? If you are looking for a specific study course within the Study Bible (like the "13-week course on Anxiety"), I can help you find that specific outline.

Practicing the Presence of God.html:
Practicing the Presence of God is a collection of the wisdom of Brother Lawrence (born Nicolas Herman), a 17th-century lay brother in a Carmelite monastery. Because the book was compiled by his friend, Father Joseph de Beaufort, after Lawrence's death, the structure is a bit different from a modern instructional manual. It is primarily organized into Conversations and Letters. Table of Contents I. The Conversations These are records of four separate interviews Father de Beaufort had with Brother Lawrence between 1666 and 1667.

  • First Conversation: The basic principles of his spiritual life and the "habitual silent conversation" with God.
  • Second Conversation: Dealing with spiritual dryness and finding God in the "ordinary" (even in the kitchen).
  • Third Conversation: Trusting God during times of sickness and physical pain.
  • Fourth Conversation: A summary of his spiritual method and the importance of a "total surrender" to God's will.
  • II. The Letters
  • This section contains 15 or 16 letters (depending on the edition) written by Brother Lawrence to various people, including nuns and friends, offering practical advice on staying connected to God.
  • Letters 1–5: Focus on the beginning stages of practicing God's presence and overcoming distractions.
  • Letters 6–10: Address maintaining peace of mind and the necessity of faith over feeling.
  • Letters 11–16: Offer encouragement for those suffering from illness or spiritual exhaustion.
  • III. Spiritual Maxims (Optional Section)
  • Many editions include a section titled "Spiritual Maxims," which consists of Brother Lawrence's core "rules" for the interior life.
  • The Goal of the Spiritual Life: Union with God.
  • The Means to Attain It: Sincerity, simplicity, and constant awareness.

Rocket Propulsion Elements.html:
The definitive text you're looking for is "Rocket Propulsion Elements" by George P. Sutton (often co-authored with Oscar Biblarz and, in the latest edition, James H. Morehart). It is widely considered the "bible" of rocket engineering. Below is the table of contents for the 10th Edition, which reflects the most current advancements in the field: Rocket Propulsion Elements (10th Edition) Foundations & Fundamentals

  • Chapter 1: Classification – Overview of jet propulsion, rocket propulsion, and various energy sources (chemical, nuclear, solar, electric).
  • Chapter 2: Definitions and Fundamentals – Core terminology, thrust, exhaust velocity, and energy efficiencies.
  • Chapter 3: Nozzle Theory and Thermodynamic Relations – Ideal systems, isentropic flow, nozzle configurations (cone vs. bell), and real-world losses.
  • Chapter 4: Flight Performance – Gravity-free and atmospheric flight, orbital mechanics, and mission analysis.
  • Chemical Rocket Propulsion
  • Chapter 5: Chemical Rocket Propellant Performance Analysis – Thermochemical calculations and computer-assisted analysis.
  • Chapter 6: Liquid Propellant Rocket Engine Fundamentals – Feed systems, tank pressurization, and engine cycles.
  • Chapter 7: Liquid Propellants – Properties of oxidizers, fuels, and monopropellants; safety and environmental concerns.
  • Chapter 8: Thrust Chambers – Injectors, combustion chamber design, materials, and heat transfer analysis.
  • Chapter 9: Liquid Propellant Combustion and its Stability – Analysis of combustion processes and control of instabilities.
  • Chapter 10: Turbopumps and their Gas Supplies – Pump and turbine configurations and design approaches.
  • Chapter 11: Engine Systems, Controls, and Integration – System calibration, optimization, and propellant budgeting.
  • Solid & Hybrid Systems
  • Chapter 12: Solid-Propellant Rocket Motor Fundamentals – Burning rates and grain configuration/stress analysis.
  • Chapter 13: Solid Rocket Propellants – Classification, hazards, and manufacturing processes.
  • Chapter 14: Solid Propellant Combustion and its Stability – Ignition, extinction, and instability.
  • Chapter 15: Solid Rocket Motor Components and Design – Motor cases, nozzles, and igniter hardware.
  • Chapter 16: Hybrid Propellants Rocket Propulsion – Applications and performance analysis of systems using combined phases.
  • Advanced Concepts & Testing
  • Chapter 17: Electric Propulsion – Electrothermal, electrostatic, and electromagnetic thrusters.
  • Chapter 18: Thrust Vector Control – Mechanisms for steering and integration with flight vehicles.
  • Chapter 19: Selection of Rocket Propulsion Systems – Criteria for choosing the right system for a specific mission.
  • Chapter 20: Rocket Exhaust Plumes – Plume appearance, flow behavior, and mathematical simulation.
  • Chapter 21: Rocket Testing – Test facilities, instrumentation, and post-accident procedures.
  • Appendices
  • Appendix 1: Conversion Factors and Constants
  • Appendix 2: Properties of the Earth's Standard Atmosphere
  • Appendix 3: Summary of Key Equations for Ideal Chemical Rockets

What it Means to be a Christian.html:
The book title "What It Means to Be a Christian" is most famously associated with Joseph Ratzinger (Pope Benedict XVI), but given your interest in structured study guides like the LifeChange series and authors like Swindoll, you might also be looking for the study by Don Stewart or a similar discipleship manual. Here are the tables of contents for the three most common works with this title:

  • What It Means to Be a Christian: Three Sermons
  • By Joseph Ratzinger (2006)
  • This is a short, profound theological work originally published as three meditations. Its table of contents is simple:
  • I. Baptism, Faith, and Membership in the Church
  • II. On the Concept of the Christian
  • III. What is the Christian?
  • What It Means to Be a Christian (Study Guide)
  • By Don Stewart (Living the Christian Life Series)
  • If you are looking for a classroom or personal study structure (similar to the LifeChange 2 Timothy study), this is likely the one. It is organized by 40 specific questions:
  • Part 1: The Basics
    • What Is a Christian?
    • How Does a Person Become a Christian? (Born Again)
    • What Happens When a Person Is “Born Again?”
    • What Are Some Misconceptions about the New Birth?
  • Part 2: God's Plan for Our Lives
    • Does God Have a Plan for Our Lives?
    • What Are the Biblical Guidelines for Knowing the Will of God?
    • What Practical Things Can We Do in Making Major Decisions?
  • Part 3: 10 Principles for Successful Christian Living
    • Principle 1: Why Do We Need a Biblical View of God?
    • Principle 2: Learning to Accept Ourselves.
    • Principle 3: Knowing What and Why We Believe.
    • Principle 4: Not Compromising Our Beliefs.
  • "So, You Want to Be Like Christ"
  • By Charles R. Swindoll (2007)
  • While not titled "What It Means to Be a Christian," this book is Swindoll’s definitive answer to that question and is often shelved under that topic. It focuses on eight essential disciplines:
  • Introduction: The Gymnasium of the Soul
  • Chapter 1: Intimacy: Deepening Our Lives
  • Chapter 2: Simplicity: Uncluttering Our Minds
  • Chapter 3: Silence and Solitude: Slowing Our Pace
  • Chapter 4: Surrender: Releasing Our Grip
  • Chapter 5: Prayer: Calling Out
  • Chapter 6: Humility: Bowing Low
  • Chapter 7: Self-Control: Holding Back
  • Chapter 8: Sacrifice: Giving Over
